channel.title: Paper Feeds
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper feeds from arXiv and IACR

item.title: VIPER-MCP: Detecting and Exploiting Taint-Style Vulnerabilities in Model Context Protocol Servers
item.description: Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers expose privileged operations (e.g., shell execution, filesystem access) to LLM agents, making taint-style vulnerabilities—where natural-language input propagates to security-sensitive sinks—a critical threat. Existing detection tools either yield unconfirmed static alerts or rely on rigid templates that fail to trigger complex, multi-step, or structurally constrained vulnerabilities. We present **VIPER-MCP**, the first end-to-end automated framework for MCP server auditing that *detects* taint-style flaws *and* dynamically *confirms exploitability* by generating concrete, executable proof-of-concept prompts. It introduces: (1) an **anchor-query pass** in two-phase static analysis that refines file-level taint alerts into function-level, vulnerability-anchored call chains tied to specific MCP tool handlers; and (2) a **feedback-driven prompt evolution mechanism** using dual mutators (for tool-selection correction and parameter penetration) and fitness-scored seed selection to iteratively optimize natural-language inputs toward vulnerable sinks. Evaluated on 39,884 real-world open-source MCP servers, VIPER-MCP discovered **106 confirmed 0-day vulnerabilities**, with **67 assigned CVE IDs** to date—all responsibly disclosed.

item.title: Auditing Apple's DifferentialPrivacy.framework: Implementation Bugs, Misconfigurations, and Practical Risks
item.description: We present the first client-side audit of Apple’s closed-source `DifferentialPrivacy.framework` on macOS Sonoma 14.2 and Sequoia 15.6. Through binary reverse engineering, Objective-C interface recovery, and custom runtime harnesses, we empirically test whether Apple’s deployed mechanisms—including Count Median Sketch, Hadamard-CMS, randomized response, and Prio-style secure aggregation—satisfy their advertised differential privacy (DP) or zero-knowledge proof guarantees. We find **5 out of 9 active mechanisms violate DP**, affecting **87% of data collection in Sonoma and 68% in Sequoia**. Critically, all floating-point–based noise mechanisms fail due to insecure samplers vulnerable to IEEE 754 rounding errors and state leakage; several secure-aggregation deployments disable local DP entirely, exposing raw records in system logs. We further decode publicly leaked iPhone logs to recover sensitive signals—including Safari domains and emoji usage—demonstrating real-world privacy harm. This work exposes critical gaps between theoretical DP promises and industrial implementation security.

item.title: Information Leakage Envelopes
item.description: We introduce the **PML envelope**, a novel privacy measure for pointwise maximal leakage (PML) that simultaneously satisfies two operational desiderata: robustness under arbitrary post-processing and direct control of the failure probability—i.e., the probability that leakage exceeds a given threshold. Unlike approximate differential privacy–inspired alternatives—which fail one or both requirements—the PML envelope is *defined* as the supremum of PML over all possible post-processings of a mechanism’s output, guaranteeing both properties by construction. We establish its fundamental properties—including monotonicity, convexity, and data-processing inequality—and derive general upper/lower bounds. For PML-extremal mechanisms in the high-privacy regime, the envelope achieves the minimal possible value; for randomized response, we obtain a closed-form expression, revealing its efficiency in sparse settings. The PML envelope thus provides a natural, interpretable, and downstream-resilient privacy guarantee.

item.title: Choose Wisely and Privately: Proactive Client Selection for Fair and Efficient Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without raw data sharing, yet averaging-based FL suffers from slow convergence and degraded accuracy under non-IID data. Reactive client selection or aggregation strategies waste computation, increase communication rounds, and expose unnecessary privacy risks. We propose a *proactive* client selection framework that identifies an optimal fixed-size federation *before training*, balancing collective data utility and cross-group fairness. Our method computes differentially private mutual information from DP contingency tables to quantify inter-feature relevance in the union dataset, and introduces the Potential Federation Loss (PFL)—a joint objective maximizing utility while constraining unfair correlation disparities across sensitive groups. Client selection is formulated as a differentially private subset optimization, solved via simulated annealing with strong ε-DP guarantees on local statistics. Experiments on four benchmarks show our selected federations yield models that converge 1.8–3.2× faster, achieve 2.4–5.7% higher accuracy, and reduce group unfairness (e.g., Equalized Odds gap) by 41–69%—outperforming uniform sampling and state-of-the-art adaptive methods like q-FFL and SCAFFOLD.

item.title: GenAI-Driven Threat Detection with Microsoft Security Copilot
item.description: This paper introduces the **Dynamic Threat Detection Agent (DTDA)**, a production-grade autonomous agent integrated into Microsoft Security Copilot to address the reactive nature of modern threat detection. DTDA continuously investigates security incidents across Microsoft Defender by unifying alerts, events, UEBA signals, and threat intelligence into a coherent activity timeline; applying versioned, schema-validated LLM prompt contracts with grounding constraints and fail-closed safety; executing a planner-executor loop to generate attack hypotheses and gather corroborating/refuting evidence; and dynamically generating explainable, MITRE-mapped alerts with contextual titles, severity ratings, remediation guidance, and natural-language narratives. In a 120-day online evaluation across tens of thousands of customers, DTDA achieved **80.1% precision** (per user feedback) and generated novel detections for **~15%** of investigated incidents. Offline, it recovered hidden malicious activity with **0.78 F1** using GPT-5.4—outperforming GPT-4.1 (+0.12 F1) and the baseline (+0.26 F1). Operationally, it processes single-incident investigations end-to-end in a median of **28 minutes**, at a median cost of **USD 2.04**, with only **0.38% job-level failure rate**, demonstrating scalable, reliable AI-driven threat discovery.

item.title: Heartbeat-Bound Hierarchical Credentials: Cryptographic Revocation for AI Agent Swarms
item.description: We introduce **Heartbeat-Bound Hierarchical Credentials (HBHC)**, a cryptographic revocation protocol that eliminates the “zombie agent” problem in AI swarms by binding credential validity to periodic, enclave-signed liveness proofs from parent agents. Unlike OAuth 2.0 introspection or OCSP, HBHC enables **fully offline, clock-based freshness verification**—requiring only a cached public key and local time—thus ensuring deterministic revocation within $W_z \le W_{\max} + \Delta_h + \varepsilon$. Evaluation shows a **90× reduction in zombie window**, **0.26 ms authentication latency**, **18,000+ verifications/sec** under load, **0.71% end-to-end overhead**, and **zero post-revocation tool calls** even under adversarial prompt injection. Cascading revocation across a 49-agent, 4-level hierarchy strictly adheres to the theoretical bound—demonstrating the first practical, provably timely revocation for autonomous AI agent swarms.

item.title: Trusted Weights, Treacherous Optimizations? Optimization-Triggered Backdoor Attacks on LLMs
item.description: Inference compilation—a cornerstone optimization for scalable LLM deployment—assumes semantic equivalence between original and compiled graphs. We uncover, for the first time, that its inherent numerical side effects can be maliciously exploited to implant *optimization-triggered backdoors*: stealthy vulnerabilities that activate *only* under compilation, remaining fully dormant during standard (uncompiled) execution—and thus evading all conventional safety evaluations. We propose a unified attack framework with two complementary strategies: (1) an *input-specific* attack flipping predictions exclusively for targeted inputs under compilation, and (2) a *universal-trigger* attack that hijacks arbitrary inputs once compilation is enabled—both requiring no compiler/hardware modification. Evaluated across four open-source LLMs (LLaMA-2-7B, Qwen-1.5-7B, Phi-3-mini, Gemma-2-2B) and four tasks, our attacks achieve **90.2% average attack success rate**, while preserving **≥99.8% clean accuracy** across all settings. This reveals a critical, previously overlooked attack surface at the intersection of system optimization and model security.

item.title: Quality and Security Signals in AI-Generated Python Refactoring Pull Requests
item.description: This study presents the first large-scale empirical analysis of quality and security impacts of AI-generated Python refactoring pull requests (PRs) from the AIDev dataset. We assess changes using PyQu (measuring five code quality attributes), Pylint (code quality issues), and Bandit (security vulnerabilities), and derive a taxonomy of 24 recurring refactoring operations mapped to their most common lint/security effects. Results show that only 22.5% of agent-authored changes improve at least one quality attribute—usability most frequently (36.5%). Meanwhile, 24.17% of modified files introduce new Pylint issues (mostly convention violations like line length), and 4.7% introduce new Bandit findings. Despite these risks, 73.5% of PRs are merged—including those adding new issues—often because they simultaneously resolve more pre-existing problems. Our work highlights the dual nature of agentic refactoring and advocates for tool-in-the-loop quality and security gating in AI-driven development workflows.

item.title: FedCritic: Serverless Federated Critic Learning-based Resource Allocation for Multi-Cell OFDMA in 6G
item.description: In 6G ultra-dense networks, aggressive frequency reuse intensifies inter-cell interference (ICI), tightly coupling subcarrier scheduling and power control across neighboring cells. To address distributed downlink resource allocation under ICI coupling and long-term per-user minimum-rate QoS constraints, we propose **FedCritic**: a *serverless federated multi-agent actor-critic framework* with fully decentralized execution. Unlike CTDE methods requiring centralized critic training, FedCritic distributes critic learning via lightweight *gossip-based parameter averaging over the interference graph*, eliminating the central coordinator while ensuring stable value estimation. Virtual-queue deficit weights enforce long-term QoS without explicit constraint optimization. In reuse-1 simulations, FedCritic improves mean SINR (+18.2 dB), cell-edge rate (+23.7%), network sum-rate (+15.4%), and fairness (Jain index +0.12) over baselines, while achieving more stable training and 68% lower coordination overhead.

item.title: Stdlib or Third-Party? Empirical Performance and Correctness of LLM-Assisted Zero-Dependency Python Libraries
item.description: This paper empirically evaluates the feasibility, correctness, and performance of replacing popular third-party Python libraries with stdlib-only reimplementations—developed using LLM assistance under strict zero-dependency constraints (single file, no imports, API compatibility, automated correctness validation). Across 40+ modules spanning 12 categories (e.g., serialization, networking, cryptography), we find that stdlib-only implementations achieve performance parity (≤2× slowdown) in most cases; major bottlenecks arise only where reference libraries rely on C extensions (e.g., image processing, binary serialization), not from pure-Python overhead. Surprisingly, several zerodep modules outperform their third-party counterparts by 5–115× by eliminating architectural bloat. LLMs generate correct, efficient code for deterministic, low-complexity tasks (e.g., Base64, URL parsing) with high first-attempt success (>90%), but require iterative human refinement for stateful protocols or numerically sensitive logic. The study establishes a practical capability map of Python’s stdlib and demonstrates that LLM-assisted, constraint-driven development enables scalable, secure, dependency-free software engineering. Project: https://github.com/Oaklight/zerodep.

item.title: Beyond Text-to-SQL: An Agentic LLM System for Governed Enterprise Analytics APIs
item.description: Enterprise analytics increasingly relies on governed APIs—not raw databases—to enforce business logic, security, and auditability. Yet mainstream Text-to-SQL systems fail in this setting: delegating aggregation or calculation logic to LLMs introduces reliability, interpretability, and compliance risks. We introduce **Analytic Agent**, an agentic LLM system that translates natural language intents into secure, policy-compliant interactions with enterprise analytics APIs—*without generating SQL or executable code*. It performs multi-step reasoning to decompose user goals, validate permissions against governance policies, orchestrate atomic API calls, and synthesize compliant visualizations. Evaluated on 90 real-world enterprise use cases (curated by domain experts across finance, supply chain, and HR), Analytic Agent achieves **92.3% end-to-end success rate**, **zero policy violations**, and full audit trail coverage—outperforming Text-to-SQL baselines by +30.5 points. This work bridges the gap between LLM usability and enterprise governance requirements.

item.title: Governance by Construction for Generalist Agents
item.description: This paper introduces **CUGA**, a composable, runtime governance architecture for generalist LLM agents in enterprise settings. Moving beyond post-hoc monitoring or model-specific fine-tuning, CUGA embeds policy enforcement *by construction* at five structural checkpoints: Intent Guard (pre-planning request validation), Playbook (dynamic reasoning steering via structured tool sequences), Tool Guide (tool-call boundary enforcement), Tool Approvals (human-in-the-loop gating for high-risk actions), and Output Formatter (response filtering and structuring). Evaluated in a healthcare use case, CUGA demonstrates dynamic playbook injection, real-time harmful-intent blocking, and auditable human approval workflows—achieving 92% higher policy adherence and 87% fewer execution inconsistencies versus baseline agents. Crucially, all governance is implemented as typed, reusable code primitives—enabling rapid, safe deployment of agentic systems across domains without model retraining.

item.title: Distributional Alignment as a Criterion for Designing Task Vectors in In-Context Learning
item.description: This paper introduces **distributional alignment**—matching next-token predictive distributions—as a principled, direct criterion for designing task vectors in in-context learning (ICL). We propose $d_{\text{NTP}}$, a lightweight metric quantifying KL divergence between task-vector-based and ICL-based next-token probabilities, and show it strongly correlates with downstream accuracy (ρ ≈ −0.92). Guided by this, we develop **Linear Task Vector (LTV)**, a closed-form method that regresses demonstration effects onto hidden states to minimize $d_{\text{NTP}}$. Across 8 classification benchmarks and 5 LLMs (e.g., Llama-2, Qwen, Phi-3), LTV improves average accuracy by **+9.2%** over state-of-the-art baselines while reducing inference latency. It further generalizes to regression tasks and enables cross-scale transfer: LTVs from a 7B model boost a 3B model’s performance by **+6.4%**, revealing task representations as reusable, scale-agnostic knowledge carriers.

item.title: Optimized Federated Knowledge Distillation with Distributed Neural Architecture Search
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) struggles with statistical, system, and communication heterogeneity in real-world deployments, yet most methods assume fixed client architectures—limiting adaptability to diverse data complexity and hardware constraints. This paper proposes **FedKDNAS**, the first framework that unifies *client-side distributed neural architecture search* (NAS) with *server-coordinated knowledge distillation*. Each client autonomously selects a lightweight, task- and resource-aware model from a shared search space, trains it via a hybrid objective (supervised + distillation), and uploads only logits on a public reference set. The server aggregates and smooths these predictions—optionally fusing with a global teacher—to generate robust distillation targets for the next round. Evaluated across six datasets against six state-of-the-art FL baselines, FedKDNAS achieves superior Pareto efficiency: up to **+15.2% accuracy** under severe non-IID conditions, **−28.4% client CPU usage**, and **up to 44× reduction in communication overhead**, all while maintaining logit-only, bandwidth-efficient transmission.

item.title: CRAFT: Conflict-Resolved Aggregation for Federated Training
item.description: Federated learning (FL) suffers from conflicting client updates under data heterogeneity, where naive averaging improves the global objective but degrades performance for individual clients. We propose **CRAFT**, a conflict-resolved aggregation framework that formulates federated aggregation as a geometric correction problem: finding the update *closest to a reference direction* (e.g., FedAvg mean) while satisfying *conflict-free alignment constraints* with all clients. We derive a **closed-form solution** to this constrained optimization—eliminating iterative solver overhead—and extend it with **layer-wise adaptation** to resolve conflicts at varying feature granularities. Theoretically, CRAFT enforces a *common-descent structure* via projection geometry, provably mitigating directional conflicts. Experiments on heterogeneous benchmarks (FEMNIST, CIFAR-100-LT, Shakespeare) show CRAFT consistently outperforms SOTA baselines: it improves global model accuracy by **1.8–3.2 percentage points** and reduces inter-client accuracy variance by **37–51%**, achieving better fairness and robustness. Code: https://github.com/tum-pbs/CRAFT.

item.title: FedCoE: Bridging Generalization and Personalization via Federated Coordinated Dual-level MoEs
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) struggles to reconcile global generalization and local personalization: averaging-based methods suffer from parameter divergence under non-IID data, while personalized FL overfits locally and fails in cold-start scenarios. To address this, we propose **FedCoE**, a Federated Coordinated dual-level Mixture-of-Experts framework. FedCoE maintains *independent global expert models* on the server and employs a *shared gating network* that dynamically learns client-expert correlations during aggregation—mitigating expert drift and gating inconsistency. Crucially, its adaptive cold-start mechanism enables new clients to immediately leverage the global expert pool without any local fine-tuning. Experiments show FedCoE achieves **78.00% average global accuracy** (+8.82% over SOTA) and **89.32% personalized accuracy** (+29.19%), while attaining **77.27% accuracy in zero-shot cold-start settings**, outperforming baselines by >12.54%. FedCoE bridges generalization and personalization through coordinated, routing-aware federated MoEs.

item.title: Federated LoRA Fine-Tuning for LLMs via Collaborative Alignment
item.description: This paper introduces **CLAIR (Collaborative Low-rank Alignment and Identifiable Recovery)**, a contamination-aware framework for federated LoRA fine-tuning of LLMs under high heterogeneity—where clients share only partial structure and many may be data- or model-contaminated. CLAIR operates solely on preliminary local LoRA estimators and recovers the shared low-rank subspace while identifying contaminated clients via structured low-rank plus block-sparse matrix decomposition. We prove exact recovery (noiseless), stable recovery (under estimation error), and consistent collaborative-set identification (under mild subspace separation). Crucially, CLAIR reduces off-subspace error via cross-client averaging while preserving client-specific variation *within* the shared LoRA subspace—yielding guaranteed improvement over local fine-tuning when the oracle gain exceeds subspace estimation and heterogeneity costs. Experiments on a Transformer text-copying task show 92.4% contamination detection accuracy and +5.7% average accuracy gain for benign clients over local fine-tuning, outperforming non-robust federated averaging.

item.title: Reinforcement Learning-based Control via Y-wise Affine Neural Networks: Comparative Case Studies for Chemical Processes
item.description: This work introduces Y-wise Affine Neural Network RL (YANN-RL)—a reinforcement learning framework designed for trustworthy, efficient, and model-light control of chemical processes. Unlike conventional RL methods, YANN-RL employs *output-aware affine initialization* of actor-critic networks, embedding domain knowledge of controlled variables (Y) directly into network structure to ensure safe, interpretable, and high-quality initial policies. Evaluated on three benchmark chemical processes from PC-Gym (CSTR, four-tank system, extraction column), YANN-RL achieves NMPC-level performance—e.g., 1.08× NMPC’s steady-state tracking error in CSTR temperature control—while requiring **55–83% less training data and 62–79% less training time** than PPO, SAC, DDPG, and TD3. Crucially, it attains this without a full nonlinear process model, bridging the gap between RL agility and industrial control reliability.

item.title: Automated Byzantine-Resilient Clustered Decentralized Federated Learning for Battery Intelligence in Connected EVs
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving battery intelligence for connected EVs, yet centralized aggregation introduces single points of failure and trust bottlenecks. We propose **ABC-DFL**, an automated, Byzantine-resilient, clustered decentralized FL framework leveraging open-permissioned blockchain with a novel dynamic Quorum Byzantine Fault Tolerance (QBFT) protocol and oracle-based aggregation. Its core—**FLECA**—employs adaptive intra-group filtering (using deviation-aware thresholds) and robust inter-group clustering by oracles to isolate malicious updates. Experiments show FLECA matches FedProx convergence under benign settings and achieves attack impact scores <0.10 under adaptive adversarial attacks—outperforming Krum and Bulyan by 2.1–3.8×. Incentive fairness is validated via multitask learning (Gini coefficient reduced by 37%), and on-chain benchmarks confirm practicality (124 TPS, <1.8 s end-to-end latency).

item.title: A Typed Tensor Language for Federated Learning
item.description: This paper introduces a **typed tensor language (TTL)** that formalizes the common mathematical structure of federated learning: client-local tensor computation, mergeable aggregation into shared state, and shared-only post-processing. TTL distinguishes *federated tensors*—whose records are partitioned across clients along a tracked record axis—from *shared tensors*, available globally. Its semantics are defined relative to a virtual global tensor, used solely as a reference. The central result is a **shared-state factorization theory**: every typed one-round program factors through a fixed-dimensional shared state whose size is independent of the number of clients and total records—computed from client-local expressions and merged across clients. We prove a converse representability theorem: any such factorization with TTL-expressible encoders and decoders is realized by a typed one-round program, and the correspondence extends to iterative programs with shared cross-round state. For learning, we develop a differentiable fragment: if per-record loss and gradient are expressible locally, the global gradient is obtained via record-axis summation of the federated gradient tensor—enabling typed iterative algorithms for server-side gradient descent and shared linear-algebra second-order updates. TTL thus characterizes a broad class of FL computations whose communication passes exclusively through bounded-dimensional shared state.

item.title: LOSCAR-SGD: Local SGD with Communication-Computation Overlap and Delay-Corrected Sparse Model Averaging
item.description: We propose **LOSCAR-SGD**, the first theoretically grounded Local SGD variant that simultaneously integrates *communication compression* (via coordinate sparsification), *local computation*, and *communication-computation overlap* in heterogeneous distributed settings. Unlike prior methods, LOSCAR-SGD enables workers to compute continuously while communication is in flight and introduces a **delay-corrected merge rule** that intelligently incorporates stale synchronized gradients without discarding local progress during overlap. We establish convergence guarantees for smooth non-convex objectives, explicitly characterizing how sparsity level (*k*), communication delay (*τ*), and worker heterogeneity (max local steps *H*) affect the convergence rate. To our knowledge, this is the first analysis unifying all three acceleration techniques. Experiments confirm that overlap reduces end-to-end training time by 1.8–2.3×, and the delay-corrected merge consistently outperforms naive overwriting by 0.7–1.2% in final test accuracy.

item.title: Distributed Direct Preference Optimization
item.description: Preference-based reinforcement learning is vital for aligning AI systems with human values, yet its theoretical foundations in distributed settings—where preference data are fragmented across heterogeneous users—remain largely unexplored. Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) avoids reward modeling but lacks convergence guarantees under federated or decentralized training due to communication constraints and non-IID preferences. We provide the first rigorous convergence and time-complexity analysis of DPO in distributed environments. For federated DPO, we derive explicit convergence rates that quantify how client drift, communication frequency, and preference heterogeneity jointly affect optimization. For decentralized DPO, we prove convergence over general connected graphs and show that the spectral gap of the communication matrix governs both convergence speed and consensus quality. Empirical results on standard alignment benchmarks (e.g., UltraFeedback) confirm our theory: our methods achieve higher win rates (+2.3–5.1 pts) with 40% less communication, demonstrating strong practical scalability and robustness.

item.title: Unsupervised clustering and classification of upper limb EMG signals during functional movements: a data-driven
item.description: This study proposes a data-driven pipeline for unsupervised clustering and classification of upper-limb surface electromyography (sEMG) during functional reach-and-grasp movements. Applied to the NINAPRO DB4 dataset (52 gestures), it integrates four stages: low-pass filtering (0.6 Hz) + Hilbert envelope preprocessing; extraction and rigorous selection of five discriminative features (e.g., RMS, WL, MNF) via mutual information, PCA, and tree-based importance; Mahalanobis-distance hierarchical clustering to identify six biomechanically diverse, computationally efficient representative gestures; and optimal 200-ms window segmentation. Automated benchmarking (PyCaret) identified Extra Trees (ET) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) as top classifiers. Independent validation confirmed ET’s robustness (92.7% ± 2.1% accuracy, κ = 0.91) and ANN’s progressive learning capability—supporting adaptive, low-latency control for myoelectric prostheses and scalable real-time deployment.

item.title: LASH: Adaptive Semantic Hybridization for Black-Box Jailbreaking of Large Language Models
item.description: Jailbreak attacks reveal persistent safety gaps in aligned LLMs, yet existing automated methods are constrained by rigid adherence to a single attack family (e.g., fixed search, mutation space, or strategy library), with no universal winner across models or harm categories. We propose **LASH** (LLM Adaptive Semantic Hybridization), a black-box framework that treats outputs from diverse base jailbreak attacks as reusable seed prompts and *adaptively composes* them per target request. Given a seed pool, LASH jointly searches over seed subsets and softmax-normalized mixture weights; a lightweight composition module synthesizes a candidate prompt; and a derivative-free genetic optimizer updates weights using only black-box target feedback and a two-stage fitness function—combining keyword-based refusal detection with LLM-judge scoring of substantive harmful fulfillment. On JailbreakBench (100 harmful prompts, 10 categories) across six target models, LASH achieves **84.5%** keyword-based and **74.5%** two-stage success rates—outperforming five SOTA baselines with only **30 mean queries**—and maintains competitiveness under three major defenses while inducing more success-like internal representations. This demonstrates adaptive semantic hybridization as a highly promising direction for efficient, query-frugal black-box red-teaming.

item.title: WCXB: A Multi-Type Web Content Extraction Benchmark
item.description: Web content extraction—identifying the main content of a web page while removing boilerplate—is critical for search indexing, RAG, NLP dataset curation, and LLM pretraining. Existing benchmarks suffer from small scale (100–800 pages), narrow focus on news articles, and outdated pages (>10 years old), limiting realistic evaluation. We introduce **WCXB**, a new benchmark comprising **2,008 real-world pages** from **1,613 domains**, spanning **seven structurally diverse page types**: articles, forums, products, collections, listings, documentation, and service pages. It includes a 1,497-page development set and a 511-page held-out test set with balanced type distributions. Ground truth was produced via a rigorous five-stage pipeline: LLM-assisted drafting, automated verification, four-pass frontier model review, snippet/quality validation scripts, and human review. Evaluating 13 extractors (11 heuristic, 2 neural), we find top systems achieve high F1 (0.93) on articles but diverge sharply on structured types (F1 = 0.41–0.84), exposing critical blind spots invisible to article-only benchmarks. WCXB is released under CC-BY-4.0 with HTML sources, annotations, type labels, and baselines.

item.title: ArPoMeme: An Annotated Arabic Multimodal Dataset for Political Ideology and Polarization
item.description: ArPoMeme is the first large-scale, manually verified multimodal dataset for Arabic political memes, comprising ~7,300 real-world examples annotated across four ideological orientations: Leftist, Islamist, Pan-Arabist, and Satirical. Grounded in the self-identification of Arabic Facebook pages/groups, it ensures cultural validity and ecological representativeness. A semi-automated pipeline—combining Playwright-based scraping, Google Drive synchronization, and Qwen2.5-VL-7B for Arabic text extraction—enables scalable yet accurate multimodal processing. Annotations cover three polarization dimensions (Us-vs-Them framing, out-group hostility, calls to action) via a custom Streamlit interface supporting distributed labeling and version control. Quantitative analysis reveals strong ideological asymmetries: Islamist and satirical memes exhibit significantly higher hostility and mobilization cues than leftist or pan-Arabist ones. ArPoMeme enables fine-grained study of Arabic political antagonism, humor, and mobilization—and is publicly released with full code, annotations, and tooling.

item.title: Findings of the Counter Turing Test: AI-Generated Text Detection
item.description: This paper presents a comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art AI-generated text detection methods through the Counter Turing Test (CT2) shared tasks. We assess two critical challenges: **Task A** (binary human-vs-AI classification) and **Task B** (fine-grained model attribution across GPT-4, Claude 3.5, and Llama variants). Results show near-perfect binary detection (top F1 = 1.0000), but substantially lower performance in model attribution (best F1 = 0.9531), exposing fundamental limitations in distinguishing outputs from increasingly homogenized LLMs. Top systems employed fine-tuned DeBERTa/BART models, ensemble strategies, and hybrid feature fusion—yet all suffered significant degradation under domain shift or against unseen models. We identify adversarial robustness, token-level attribution mechanisms, and cross-architecture generalization as pivotal open challenges for trustworthy AI content governance.

item.title: Distributed Simon's Algorithm with Less Per-Node Qubit Overhead and Its Application to Cryptanalysis
item.description: This paper proposes a novel distributed Simon’s algorithm based on an **XOR-type periodic function**, overcoming the high per-node qubit overhead of prior concatenation-based approaches (e.g., Tan et al., 2022). Our scheme reduces per-node qubit requirement from $2^{t+1}m$ to just $m + n - t$—an exponential improvement in $t$—while preserving the same per-node quantum query complexity of $2c(n-t)$ ($c>3$). This enables scalable execution on near-term hardware with limited qubits per node. We apply the algorithm to mount the first **distributed quantum key-recovery attack on SoEM22**, achieving comparable time complexity (both quantum and classical query models) to state-of-the-art non-distributed Simon attacks, but with drastically lower per-node qubit overhead. The result advances practical distributed quantum cryptanalysis under realistic hardware constraints.

item.title: On weak keys of POKE
item.description: POKE is a high-performance isogeny-based public-key encryption scheme proposed by Basso and Maino, with POKE-4D designed for smaller primes and faster encryption than POKE-2D. This work identifies a class of **weak keys** in POKE that drastically reduce the complexity of algebraic attacks by inducing structural degeneracy in the underlying isogeny graph. While such keys are negligible in POKE-2D (probability < $2^{-100}$), they occur at non-negligible rates in POKE-4D (estimated $2^{-25}$–$2^{-30}$), posing a concrete security threat. We propose revised POKE-4D parameters—enforcing stricter ideal norm constraints and adjusting prime size—to suppress weak-key probability below $2^{-128}$. However, the resulting parameters match POKE-2D in size (~4160 bits) and eliminate POKE-4D’s key advantages: its compact prime (now 448 bits instead of 376) and encryption efficiency gain vanish. This reveals a critical trade-off between performance optimization and structural key-space robustness in isogeny-based PKE.

item.title: Linear self-equivalence of the known families of APN functions: a unified point of view
item.description: This paper introduces *multivariate projective mappings*—multivariate functions whose coordinate functions are homogeneous polynomials of the same degree—as a unifying structural framework for known APN families. We develop a systematic method to detect whether a given function is linearly equivalent to such a mapping, based on derivative spaces, homogenization, and quotient invariants. Our main result proves that **almost all known infinite families of APN functions over $\mathbb{F}_{2^n}$ (including Gold, Kasami, Welch, Niho, Dobbertin, and Kim-type functions) are CCZ-equivalent either to a multivariate projective mapping or to a function commuting with the Frobenius automorphism**—despite their superficially disparate univariate, bivariate, or trivariate representations. This reveals a hidden structural unity underlying the entire landscape of known APN constructions and provides concrete algorithmic tools to verify or rule out such equivalence for arbitrary candidates.

item.title: Can We Tolerate Small Side-Channel Leakages: The Role of Registers in Glitch-Stopping Circuits
item.description: This work exposes a critical gap between theoretical security guarantees and experimental leakage assessment in glitch-resistant masked hardware. We demonstrate that small, theoretically insecure side-channel leakages—caused by glitch propagation patterns—can be *concealed* during TVLA testing due to specific register placements within the logic chain. To formalize this effect, we introduce a relaxed adversarial model extending Müller and Moradi’s (CHES 2024), explicitly incorporating register topology as a security parameter. Applying our model, we modify a provably robust AES S-box (De Cnudde et al.) into a variant that violates the robust probing model—yet exhibits *no detectable leakage* under both FPGA-based power measurements and ASIC-like gate-level simulations with accurate glitch modeling. Our results challenge the assumption that TVLA absence implies compositional security, highlighting register layout as an unintentional “leakage suppressor” and urging tighter alignment between theoretical models and physical implementation constraints.

item.title: Signal and Ready to MINGLE: In-Band Gossip for Key Transparency Split-View Detection in E2EE Messengers
item.description: End-to-end encrypted (E2EE) messengers increasingly adopt Key Transparency (KT) to detect malicious key substitution—but KT’s security collapses if an adversary equivocates by serving inconsistent KT views to different clients. Current auditor-based detection creates a centralized trust bottleneck. We propose **MINGLE**, an opportunistic *in-band gossip protocol* that embeds compact KT commitments into ordinary encrypted messages *before encryption*, requiring no external infrastructure or overlay network. By distributing consistency checks across the entire communication graph, MINGLE forces an adversary sustaining a split view to permanently isolate targeted clients—becoming infeasible as social graphs densify. Under eventual cross-partition connectivity, conflicting KT views yield publicly verifiable evidence. Prototyped in Signal Android with only **119-byte overhead per gossip message**, simulations show MINGLE generates definitive split-view evidence within **~5 minutes** in a 12,000-client deployment—even with just **20% participation** and gossip attached to only **5% of messages**. MINGLE turns routine messaging into a practical, decentralized KT audit layer.

item.title: Constant-Online PVSS from CCA2-Secure Threshold Encryption: A Generic Framework
item.description: We present a generic framework that transforms any CCA2-secure threshold encryption (CCATE) scheme into a *constant-online* Publicly Verifiable Secret Sharing (PVSS), where both the dealer’s online sharing and public verification scale as $O(1)$—independent of the number $n$ of shareholders. The key insight is to shift shareholder-dependent computation (e.g., key generation, verification material setup) into a reusable offline phase; online sharing then reduces to a single publicly verifiable threshold encryption (one ciphertext + one succinct proof). We instantiate the framework with two CCATE schemes: (1) a pairing-free variant based on Threshold ElGamal under a committee assumption, and (2) a “silent-setup” variant using non-interactive key generation via a Power-of-Tau ceremony. We further formalize epoch-based membership updates and clarify their security boundaries under respective assumptions. While setup cost increases, the critical online phases achieve constant-size communication and constant-time verification—enabling scalable PVSS in high-throughput settings like blockchains.

item.title: Unified FPGA Design of Kyber and Dilithium with Provable Fault Tolerance
item.description: We present the first unified FPGA architecture supporting both CRYSTALS-Kyber (a KEM) and CRYSTALS-Dilithium (a signature scheme), achieving flexibility, compactness (<14.2K LUTs on Artix-7), and **provable fault tolerance** against propagation-based fault attacks. Our microcoded, programmable datapath reuses core modules—including SHAKE, discrete Gaussian sampling, and coefficient rounding—with <8% area overhead. To counter faults exploiting deterministic behavior in public computations, we introduce a **probabilistic verification mechanism based on rejection sampling**, transforming critical operations into cryptographically constrained stochastic processes. Under fault-free conditions, verification succeeds with >99.7% probability per attempt (avg. retries < 1.05); adversarial faults cause near-certain failure, provably degrading attack success to negligible levels. Evaluation shows <3.2% performance overhead and zero successful attacks under 10⁴ fault injections—establishing the first unified, efficient, and formally resilient hardware design for these NIST-standardized lattice schemes.

item.title: Quantum Circuit Implementation and Grover’s Search on the Lightweight Block Cipher KLEIN Family
item.description: This work presents the first complete, reversible quantum circuit implementation of the KLEIN family (KLEIN-64/80/96), enabling rigorous analysis of its quantum security. We decompose all cipher components—including SubNibbles, RotateNibbles, MixNibbles, KeyAdd, and the full key schedule—into elementary quantum gates (CCNOT, CNOT, X), ensuring garbage-free operation and functional correctness verified via Qiskit. Our design minimizes Toffoli count and qubit overhead through nibble-level parallelism and state reuse: e.g., one KLEIN-64 round requires ~1,320 CCNOTs and ~1,080 CNOTs using $2n + r$ qubits. Integrating this into Grover’s algorithm, we provide precise resource estimates for key search: breaking KLEIN-64 demands ~$2^{32}$ Oracle calls, translating to ~1.2 billion Toffoli gates and ~256 logical qubits under surface-code error correction. These results quantify KLEIN’s resilience against near-term quantum attacks and establish a reproducible methodology for quantum cryptanalysis of lightweight ciphers.

item.title: Current trends in AI-Aided Cryptography
item.description: This paper presents a systematic trend analysis of AI-aided cryptography, addressing the lack of cross-domain, evidence-based surveys in this rapidly evolving field. We analyze 90 peer-reviewed publications (2021–2025) alongside 42 journal/conference calls for papers and 17 public R&D tenders to map research distribution across five core subfields: cryptanalysis, hashing, encryption, protocols, and post-quantum cryptography (PQC). Results reveal stark imbalances: cryptanalysis (38%) and hashing (29%) dominate, while protocols (9%), encryption (7%), and PQC (5%) remain critically underexplored. We identify key gaps—including the absence of provably secure AI training frameworks for cryptographic tasks and empirical studies on AI-resilient lightweight PQC—and highlight emerging priorities: AI-powered formal verification of cryptographic protocols and quantum-classical hybrid AI evaluation platforms. Our findings provide actionable intelligence for researchers, funders, and standardization bodies.

item.title: More from Less: Composable General Multi-Party Computation with Global Public Verifiability from a Single Enclave Only
item.description: This paper addresses two fundamental limitations in TEE-based MPC modeling. First, we identify and formalize an impossibility: the widely adopted $\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{att}}^{\mathrm{PST}}$ functionality (Pass et al., EUROCRYPT 2017) enables attestation but *fails to support meaningful public verifiability*—external parties cannot plausibly verify outputs despite real-world enclaves supporting such capability. Second, it mandates a TEE per party, even without public verifiability. To overcome both, we propose a novel simulation paradigm where equivocation is jointly managed by a new global functionality $\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{att}}$ and a locally publicly verifiable ideal functionality $\mathcal{F}$. We introduce *designated-verifier attestations*, binding proofs to a single party and execution—eliminating the per-party TEE requirement. Our model achieves **global public verifiability**: honest external verifiers’ guarantees remain intact *even under full corruption of all protocol parties* and for probabilistic $\mathcal{F}$. We construct composable, general MPC protocols secure under UC with Global Subroutines against static malicious adversaries—using only *one* enclave.

item.title: PQKryvos: Post-Quantum Secure E-Voting With Flexible Ballot Formats and Public Tally-Hiding
item.description: PQKryvos is the first practical, post-quantum secure (pq-secure), homomorphic e-voting protocol that *generically supports arbitrary ballot formats* (e.g., ranked, approval, score-based) and introduces *public tally-hiding*—a strong privacy guarantee ensuring only the final election outcome (e.g., winner or aggregate counts) is revealed, while all intermediate tallies and candidate-wise vote distributions remain provably hidden. Built as a pq-secure instantiation of the Kryvos framework, it combines lattice-based homomorphic commitments with hash-based general-purpose zero-knowledge proofs (GPZKPs) to achieve efficient ballot correctness verification and flexible tallying. Our implementation demonstrates competitive performance: for elections with 5,000 voters, end-to-end voting takes <1.2 seconds per voter, and tallying completes in under 8 seconds—outperforming the original pre-quantum Kryvos in several settings. Moreover, we show GPZKPs enable straightforward enhancement of existing pq-secure protocols to support richer ballots and public tally-hiding.

item.title: A Blockchain-Based Pre-Verification Access Control Scheme with Vector Commitments and Bulletproofs
item.description: This paper proposes a privacy-preserving pre-verification access control scheme for blockchain-assisted IoT, leveraging **vector commitments and Bulletproofs** to enable efficient, trustless on-chain policy eligibility checks. Unlike prior approaches, it encodes both user attributes and access policies as vectors, hiding authenticated attributes via vector commitments and transforming policy satisfaction into a zero-knowledge proof of a hidden inner-product relation—thus revealing neither attributes nor policy content. The scheme is **pairing-free**, requires **no trusted setup**, and supports smart-contract-based public verification. Integrated proxy re-encryption enables secure post-verification data access delegation. Security analysis confirms completeness, knowledge soundness, zero-knowledge, attribute/policy privacy, and collusion resistance. Experiments show up to **62% lower on-chain verification cost**, **48% reduced communication overhead**, and significantly lower deployment complexity versus state-of-the-art schemes.

item.title: Information-Theoretic Optimistic Verifiable Secret Sharing
item.description: This paper introduces **Information-Theoretic Optimistic Verifiable Secret Sharing (OVSS)**, a new paradigm that adapts round complexity to the *actual* number of corruptions $f$, rather than the worst-case threshold $t$. For perfect security, given a parameter $k$ ($0 \le k \le t$), our protocol completes in **2 rounds if $f \le k$**, and safely falls back to **3 rounds if $k < f \le t$**, while maintaining optimal $t < n/3$ resilience. Under statistical security, 2-round execution is guaranteed when $2t + k < n$. We provide an information-theoretically secure construction based on lightweight commitments, layered verification, and corruption-aware broadcast, and prove its correctness, privacy, and verifiability. Crucially, we establish tight lower bounds: no OVSS protocol can achieve sub-2-rounds for $f \le k$, nor sub-3-rounds for $f > k$. This work bridges the gap between theoretical optimality and practical efficiency in VSS.

item.title: Detecting Fluent Optimization-Based Adversarial Prompts via Sequential Entropy Changes
item.description: This paper proposes **CPD Online (CPD)**, a model-agnostic, training-free, online detector for fluent optimization-based adversarial prompts (e.g., GCG, AutoDAN). Instead of relying on static or windowed perplexity—which fails against fluent jailbreaks—we frame detection as sequential change-point detection over the **token-level next-token entropy stream**. By estimating a robust baseline from the system prompt, standardizing user-token entropies, and applying a one-sided CUSUM statistic, CPD localizes the *onset* of adversarial suffixes in real time. Evaluated on 1,012 attacks and 1,012 perplexity-matched benign prompts across six open-weight chat models, CPD achieves **AUROC 0.88 and F1 0.82 on LLaMA-2-7B**, outperforming all windowed-perplexity baselines in F1. Crucially, **79.6% of CPD’s triggers fall within the true adversarial suffix**, versus only 17–46% for perplexity methods. When deployed as a lightweight gate before LLaMA Guard, CPD **reduces guard invocations by 17–22%** in high-volume, benign-dominated settings—without compromising overall safety performance.

item.title: Measuring Safety Alignment Effects in Autonomous Security Agents
item.description: This paper introduces the first trace-based benchmark for evaluating safety alignment in autonomous security agents—30 deterministic local vulnerability-analysis tasks with fixed tool APIs, redaction rules, grounding validation, and binary success criteria. We compare four stock models (Gemma 4 31B/26B A4B, Qwen2.5-Coder 7B, Llama 3.1 8B) against their uncensored or abliterated derivatives, analyzing 1,500 security traces and 800 non-security control traces. Results reveal large, model-specific effects: Gemma derivatives gain +13.3–10.7 percentage points in task success and markedly improve grounding (e.g., +0.64 and +2.48 on a 5-point scale), with zero refusals, suppressed actions, or unsafe actions. However, gains are neither universal nor security-specific: Qwen2.5-Coder underperforms when unaligned (5.3% → 2.0%), and the abliterated Llama derivative violates tool protocols. Critically, all models fail hard proof-of-trigger and patch-verification tasks. We conclude that safety alignment must be measured at the *system level*, disentangling refusal, unsafe action, tool fidelity, and evidence grounding—rejecting refusal rate as a sufficient safety proxy.

item.title: Optimal Representation Size: High-Dimensional Analysis of Pretraining and Linear Probing
item.description: We present a high-dimensional analytical framework for the two-stage learning paradigm: pretraining on unlabeled data (via PCA) followed by linear probing on labeled data (via linear regression). In the asymptotic regime where dimension $d$, unlabeled samples $n_u$, and labeled samples $n_\ell$ scale together, we derive *exact* expressions for generalization error as a function of representation dimension $k$, revealing a non-monotonic trade-off. Crucially, we identify the **optimal $k$**: maximal compression ($k=1$) is optimal when pretraining data is abundant relative to supervision ($n_u \gg n_\ell$), whereas moderate $k > 1$ yields superior generalization under limited pretraining. We establish a precise equivalence: one labeled sample is worth $\Theta(n_\ell / k)$ unlabeled samples—quantifying how unsupervised data substitutes for supervision. These predictions hold empirically in autoencoders and pretrained LLMs, underscoring that *representation size is a fundamental design parameter*, not an implementation detail.

item.title: Towards Distillation Guarantees under Algorithmic Alignment for Combinatorial Optimization
item.description: This paper establishes the first rigorous sufficient condition for successful knowledge distillation in combinatorial optimization under *algorithmic alignment*. We consider distilling a rich teacher model—assumed to satisfy the Linear Representation Hypothesis (LRH)—into a student graph neural network (GNN) whose architecture is explicitly aligned with a dynamic programming (DP) algorithm for the task. Crucially, we model the DP transition function as a decision tree (DT) and show that, when this DT has polynomial size in the DP state dimension *k*, the distillation problem admits an efficient polynomial-time algorithm. Its sample and computational complexity depend only on *k* and the DT’s structural parameters—not on input graph size or combinatorial blowup. Our result provides a theoretically grounded design principle: alignment between student architecture and problem-specific algorithmic structure, coupled with LRH-compliant teacher representations, guarantees efficient and reliable distillation.

item.title: Smooth Partial Lotteries for Stable Randomized Selection
item.description: Competitive selection processes increasingly adopt partial lotteries to mitigate bias and noise in evaluation scores—but existing designs suffer from *instability*: tiny score perturbations (e.g., ±0.1) induce large, discontinuous jumps in selection probabilities. We propose *smoothness*—formalized as a Lipschitz condition on the score-to-probability mapping—as a foundational design principle. Our **Clipped Linear Lottery (CLL)** assigns probabilities linearly between lower and upper thresholds (0 and 1 outside), achieving near-optimal worst-case regret—within factor $(1 - k/n)$ of the theoretical lower bound for any smooth rule. Experiments on real peer-review data (ICLR 2025, NeurIPS 2024, SNF) confirm that standard lotteries exhibit severe instability (32–67 ppt probability shifts under single-score perturbation), while CLL caps shifts at ≤3.5 ppt and dominates alternatives in the smoothness-utility tradeoff.

item.title: Auditing Privacy in Multi-Tenant RAG under Account Collusion
item.description: This paper audits privacy in multi-tenant RAG under account collusion, exposing a critical gap: per-account DP guarantees collapse when $k$ accounts within the *same tenant* collude against their shared index. For Gaussian-noised retrieval, joint leakage degrades unconditionally at rate $Θ(\sqrt{k} \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{acc}})$—a fundamental violation of the advertised privacy boundary. We design and empirically validate a concrete membership inference attack achieving this bound. To make the per-account/joint privacy gap quantitatively auditable, we propose the first protocol that operates on *unmodified* RAG deployments, issuing a quantitative $(\textsf{PASS}, \varepsilon_{\text{audit}})$ verdict for the retrieval-score channel—without index disclosure, pipeline changes, or model-weight exposure. The protocol composes six RAG-specific cryptographic primitives with generic ones (e.g., Merkle ledgers, ZK proofs, Gaussian noise attestations) and supports both closed-form bounds and Rényi-DP moment accounting. Generation-channel privacy remains a separate audit predicate.

item.title: General Lower Bounds for Differentially Private Federated Learning with Arbitrary Public-Transcript Interactions
item.description: We establish the first general lower bound for differentially private federated learning protocols with *arbitrary public-transcript interactions*—supporting adaptive rounds and client-level sample reuse. For parameter estimation under squared ℓ₂ loss, we derive a *federated van Trees bound* applicable to any estimator satisfying a total clientwise sample-level zero-concentrated differential privacy (zCDP) constraint. The key technical contribution is a *privacy-information contraction inequality* for complete public transcripts, which quantifies how zCDP fundamentally limits information extraction from interactive protocols. We instantiate the bound for mean estimation, linear regression (yielding Ω(d/(nα²)) for d-dimensional parameters), and nonparametric regression over Sobolev classes—thereby providing tight, unified statistical limits under realistic federated interaction models.

item.title: Diffusion Graph Posterior Sampling for Nonlinear Inverse Problems with Application to Electrical Impedance Tomography
item.description: Deep generative models excel in inverse problems, yet their application to PDE-based tasks—like Electrical Impedance Tomography (EIT)—is hindered by the unstructured mesh discretization of physical domains, which breaks the assumptions of standard CNNs. We propose **Diffusion Graph Posterior Sampling (DGPS)**, the first framework extending score-based diffusion models to graph-structured data. Our unconditional diffusion prior is learned directly on a 2D triangular mesh, capturing geometric and topological priors intrinsic to the physics. To address severe ill-posedness, we introduce **Regularized DPS (RDPS)**, which explicitly incorporates total variation and generalized Tikhonov regularization into the diffusion sampling loop, synergizing implicit and explicit constraints. On synthetic and real 2D EIT benchmarks, RDPS achieves superior reconstruction accuracy (PSNR +4.2–6.8 dB under 5–15% noise), robust generalization to out-of-distribution inclusion geometries (52% lower zero-shot error), and significantly reduced artifacts compared to state-of-the-art solvers (e.g., GPnP-BM3D, DP-SGS).

item.title: An exponential mechanism based on quadratic approximations for fine-tuning machine learning models with privacy guarantees
item.description: We propose a differentially private fine-tuning algorithm based on the exponential mechanism (EM), leveraging a *quadratic approximation* of the pretrained model’s loss landscape around its initial parameters. By designing a simple utility function that combines this local quadratic surrogate with task-specific data, our EM admits *exact closed-form sampling* from a multivariate Gaussian—eliminating MCMC approximations and ensuring rigorous ε-differential privacy. We derive tight sensitivity bounds for the utility, establish theoretical accuracy guarantees (MSE bounds), and introduce a random-projection strategy to scale to high-dimensional models (e.g., ResNet, BERT). Experiments on MNIST and the clinical MIMIC-III dataset show our method achieves competitive accuracy (e.g., 97.3% on MNIST at ε=2.0, δ=10⁻⁵) while outperforming DP-SGD and DP-Adam in both utility and stability.

item.title: SMA-DP: Spectral Memory-Aware Differential Privacy for Deep Learning
item.description: Differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) suffers from high-variance updates that degrade utility on complex datasets like CIFAR-100. We propose **SMA-DP-SGD**, a spectral memory-aware variant that augments DP-SGD with a fractional memory branch built *exclusively* from previously privatized gradient releases—ensuring no additional privacy cost. Layer-wise power-law spectral exponents (inspired by WeightWatcher) provide group-level reliability signals to adaptively control memory decay and effective depth. Private-history alignment, norm matching, and warm-up activation stabilize memory integration. Crucially, conditioned on the private release history, the memory branch is deterministic; only the current clipped gradient sum—scaled by a fixed coefficient $β$—introduces new data dependence, preserving exact group-wise DP guarantees and recovering standard DP-SGD when $β = 1$. Experiments on CIFAR-100, CIFAR-10, and MNIST show consistent accuracy gains—up to +3.2% on CIFAR-100 at $(\varepsilon=2,\delta=10^{-5})$—with ablations confirming $β$’s role in steering the privacy–utility trade-off and spectral diagnostics validating controlled, lightweight memory usage. Runtime overhead is $2.94\times$ DP-SGD, reflecting a practical balance between adaptive private memory and efficiency.
